statement.markdown: Write a function to validate whether a given string of HTML tags is valid. A string of HTML tags is valid if it contains a series of well-formed opening and closing tags. The function should return an array of strings representing the valid tags in the order they appear in the input string.
statement.inputFormat:
A string of HTML tags, e.g. '

Hello

World

Foo

'
statement.outputFormat:
An array of strings representing the valid tags in the order they appear in the input string, e.g. ['

', '

', '', '', '

']
statement.notes: You can assume that the input string only contains well-formed HTML tags, i.e., there are no mismatched or overlapping tags. The input string may contain duplicate tags.

constraints.description: The maximum number of tags in the input string

examples.explanation: This example demonstrates a valid string of HTML tags with well-formed opening and closing tags.
This example demonstrates a valid string of HTML tags with duplicate tags.

hints.text: You can use a stack to keep track of the opening tags.

editorial.title: Validating HTML Tags
editorial.intuition: You can use a stack to keep track of the opening tags. When you encounter a closing tag, you can pop the corresponding opening tag from the stack.
editorial.bruteForce: You can use a regular expression to match the opening and closing tags. However, this approach may not be efficient for large input strings.
editorial.betterSolution: You can use a stack to keep track of the opening tags. When you encounter a closing tag, you can pop the corresponding opening tag from the stack.
editorial.optimalSolution: You can use a stack to keep track of the opening tags. When you encounter a closing tag, you can pop the corresponding opening tag from the stack. Finally, you can return the stack as the array of valid tags.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The stack-based approach ensures that the output array contains the valid tags in the order they appear in the input string.
editorial.dryRun:
Here's a step-by-step example run: input = '

Hello

World

Foo

'; output = ['

', '

', '', '', '

'];
commonMistakes: Forgetting to pop the corresponding opening tag from the stack when encountering a closing tag.
Not using a stack to keep track of the opening tags.
